text:
I hate to burst your bubble, but ...
Whether you call it wiggle, wobble, vibrate or even resonance it's MOVEMENT of the part.
If the part is moved by the wind, it is a form of wind vane - or BLADE.
That piece of wood and most definitely the piece of venetian blind fit the most basic description / definition of a BLADE - and unless they MOVE no electricity is generated.
Notwithstanding how interesting the concept is, I call CLICK BAIT!
Try to market it as having no moving parts and you'll be shot down for false advertising.

author: @johnwest7993
text: Speaker coils clearly have too few turns of wire to act as much more than a motion detector for wind. But even though there are all sorts of options for the mechanical design of resonant vibration electrical generators that will harvest 'some' energy at very low wind speeds, I get a very strong feeling that the optimal design for any actual power for the price goes right back to circular windmill blades and rotating generators, meaning that the more you think about it and experiment the more it converges on what we already have. As for low wind energy harvesting, I've been contemplating a small 'starter motor' to overcome startup inertia, but just as an experiment. I highly doubt it would be cost effective or energy viable for anything but my own applications.

text: Something I came up with is a pvc pipe at least 4' long and wrapping it in copper wire in places to make coils. Then put little magnets on an aluminum tube with an iron wire core. author: @Leberteich
text: It is misleading to claim this generator 'has no moving parts'. The pole vibrates. Vibration is movement. It is also misleading to say the device 'generates a voltage'. What you want to generate is power, that is voltage x current = Energy/time, or energy = power x time. You can make very high voltages e.g. with static charging but this holds only a small charge, so when it discharges gives power only for a very short time, hence delivers little energy. A meaningful measurement of generator output has to report at least two quantities, the open circuit voltage Voc (voltage when no current flows) and the short circuit current Isc (current flowing with zero voltage btwn the electrodes). Voc x Isc gives an upper limit for the power the generator can deliver. A better measurement is the full current/voltage characteristic in between the two extremes, open circuit and short circuit.

author: @geekchameleon
text:
I can't find any info on the original design, but several years ago I read an article on a young man who developed a reed-style generator to help the people of Haiti.  He used kite nylon in roughly 1x36 inch (2.5cm x 1m) strips stretched and held horizontal, with a magnet ~ 6 inches from the ends and coils on the frame.  He used kite fabric because it is rugged and lightweight (vibrates easily in very light wind), and was abundant and cheap in Haiti.  He open-sourced his design, and improved the system so that less than $10 of materials could charge a battery that would power an LED light 24x7 thanks to the ubiquitous wind coming onto the island.

The key variables were the length and width of the fabric (very narrow compared to length, but not too narrow), the position of the magnet on the strip (close to the end, but not so close you lose motion) and the spacing between the fabric and the coil (optimum coupling distance, ensuring the magnet and coil never physically touch even in high wind - touching wastes the kinetic potential).

Something as lightweight as kite fabric would be ideal, to harvest energy even in the lightest wind.  These strips could be stacked, and coils could be placed above and below the magnet to increase the amount of harvested electricity.  The idea could be scaled up (longer and wider strips) and solenoids could be used as the pickup coils to increase the output.


It may be possible to eliminate most of the framing in the original design by putting the magnet on one side of the ribbon and a coli on the other, and then flipping every other ribbon so each ribbon both creates motion and harvests electricity from the one above and the one below.

text: Holding a microphone in the open air will produce voltage. The problem is, if you took all the acoustical energy produced at a big rock concert and stored it, you MIGHT have enough energy to heat up a cup of coffee. However, speakers and microphones have been designed to follow the programs put it as accurately and efficiently as possible. It's like using the wrong tool for the job. It works, but not very well. Using bigger wires will produce more voltage. Ganging lots speakers in parallel is another way to get more for less. To put this in proper perspective, let me put it to you this way : We've all seen Big Ben. If you took enough microphones and ganged them all together within hearing distance of Big Ben, could you collect enough energy to ring the bell again? How any microphones would you need? Big Ben can be heard for miles. How many microphones could you pack into all that space. Millions? Billions? Build an anechoic chamber and put Big Ben inside so the reflected sound is used as well. Put all of that together. If you can ring the bell will the energy you've collected, you have just proved perpetual motion. The best microphone puts out about 900 millivolts at max output. I was a soundman for rock bands for 30 years (retired). I have wondered about this. It's amazing just how little sound a mic picks up. You could put them in a metal building. If the roof were constructed with two different metals, when the sun comes up they heat up at different rates. This would cause popping that the mics could pick up and convert. Rain and thunder will work too. Grids next to a high way picking up the traffic noise produces energy. This science has been pretty much worked out, but it's been geared towards producngmusic,not electricity. Perhaps some math wiz could prove it on paper? Then you might be able to try it out.

author: @mikesmuseum
text: The coil around a magnet in a speaker is supposed to move in and out.  That's why there is a 'foam surround' to keep that coil perfectly centered as it moves. The problem with this particular arrangement is that that coil will be skewed left or right and in and out, allowing that coil to rub up against the magnet - therefore causing what we call 'RUB' among other terms.  And that's not good.  And even if it did work properly you are going to get minimal current.  This is neither cost effective or even very functional.  You have basically made a 'wind- microphone'.  I do enjoy these videos though, well done.

Interesting idea, as a concept.
But absolutely NO WAY to get any significant or useful energy out of such a system.
Where is the energy coming from?
From the wind, from moving air! From a moving mass...
If the area of capture is minuscule, and the density (of air) is insignificant, well the output to expect is virtually ZERO !
So don't build any expectations :-(
Just think of the area swept by a modern wind turbine, and the rather strong winds expected or needed to get useful energy out !
There is no such thing, as a free meal !!  :-)

There was a type of wave power harvester that I saw a video on once.  Basically it put a buoy on the surface and had a wire running down to the ocean bed.  Ever time the buoy bobbed up and down it pulled and released tension on the wire, which powered a little piezoelectric generator on the seabed.

replies.author: @alanpolain1161
replies.text: I went to an I Mech E presentation by Salter and he seemed to regard his ducks as an interesting idea rather than a practical power generation source. It worked as waves moved past the duck (wave energy not up and down movement) and the duck rotated relative to the ducks on either side. It had plenty of practical problems as the rotational energy used hydraulic systems. Hydraulics and seawater are not a good mix for a long service life. Plus the best place to put them (off the north coast of Scotland) would also be the place guaranteed to give them the shortest service life,  and they needed to be towed back to port for repairs.

author: @Deebz270
text:
Hmm...   A speaker is a device that transforms electrical energy into *kinetic* energy (sound pressure waves - SPW).
A microphone is a device that transforms *kinetic* (SPW) energy into electrical energy.  
Anything that transforms kinetic energy, by definition, must have MOVING PARTS...  
.
So how many inductors would it take to go 'off-grid' in an average sized home?   
Then there is the spurious noise produced, mostly likely when the wind changes direction or intensity... 
So putting it on the roof as a very silly idea...  
.
In fact, the whole concept is dubious, especially when scaled up.  
.
But hey!   Waste what little money you have buying veneitan blinds and inductors etc...  So you can squeeze miniscule amounts of joules from the wind.
.
What next I wonder?   Small centrifugal pumps (like the type used to pump water down a hosepipe...) fitted with a dynamo instead of a moter and fitted inline with the house's water supply...  That way, whenever you run the tap, you can squeeze yet more joules out.... LMAO!

Give the "three-thirds" rule a shot. Anchor the reed vertically, mount the inductor 1/3rd of the way up, allow 2/3rds of the reed to operate freely in the wind, and have a bank (or banks) of several thousand reeds in staggered rows to stimulate turbulence. There was a Chinese guy in the US who set up a long rubber band and inductor system across his window... it generated enough voltage to run his electric wall clock.

author: @andyowens5494
text: Its a nice toy, but I really cant imagine getting any significant power out of that kind of arrangement - at best its going to be highly inefficient. As for the Spanish innovators, I'm sure someone will invest, but I'll put my money elsewhere - I cant imagine the physics making this even slightly economic. There might be a niche for charging a mobile phone when you're 300 miles from a socket (or research transmitters in remote locations, for which we have perfectly good solar panels which also have no moving parts and can be virtually maintenance free), but hauling a coil through a magnetic field using a turbine is always going to extract more energy than wiggling a stick in the wind.

author: @Jkirk3279
text:
Many years ago, I read an article about using VHS tape to make a blade less turbine.

Apply electricity and the tape wiggles, pushing air.

With the right piezoelectric material, a flag flapping in the wind would generate voltage.

With a LOT of flags, you could harvest power from a tiny breeze.

The ultimate version would look like a tree with streamers on it.

Unlike bladed turbines, storms wouldn’t be a threat.

author: @leefoster4133
text:
Yes mate it's quite interesting.  Let me discuss something that I came up with when I was a lad.  I was going to use this as one of my PhD papers but since I wasn't able to achieve it and the general knowledge of it was released by a Canadian; what the hell.  There is something that was referred to as a crystal battery I believe they called it.  The key ingredient is quartz crystals.  You can find it on youtube by searching “crystal battery lasersaber”.  There are other with their own take on it but I can't recall any one person getting completely right.  Also I can't remember the Canadian PhD but his humour is what I refer to as a bit off.  Smart but twisted humour to the point am not assumed.  And I can be quite twisted humour wise.  
Any how this is the physics of it all.  The quartz crystals are oscillated by multiple sources in our world today.  But back when I was working on it it was oscillated by the changes to the magnetosphere.  Which I always heard as a low frequency pitch in my ears.  People always had to get some distance and even so I still heard them.  Back to the physics at hand.  The oscillation of the magnetosphere is caused by the sun or from what is referred to as cosmic radiation.  The frequency range and I never had the equipment at the time but I theorised that it had to be in 500Mhz to low gigahertz range.  When this “crystal battery” was subject to this it would create a low voltage and current power source.  At the time it was merely a that's interesting because it was long before super caps which could be used to harness the power in more practical uses.  Say using 1 – 2 cm by 1 – 2 cm circular copper container.  Then connecting 12 of them together you could get say 12v@.1A out of it.  Not allot I know but it is a small watch like battery.  But if you feed this into some super caps to charge it up then you could charge or run something that is low wattage.  A small transistor radio was the device I was thinking about at the time but I didn't have super caps then.  Normal caps have too much leakage.
So, this is another free design of piezoelectric generation.  The only problem with it is that the crystals do grow and it does destroys the copper housing.

The improvement would be to use the "reed" as a fulcrum and have 2 opposing speakers with the cones intact but its about the stiffness of the spider and the cone surround.......so you would want to create a dual piston effect with the reed as the fulcrum and have a joint on a rod with  centering bearing so not to deviate from the linear travel of the speaker cones.  If you used opposing cones....one would tug out and the other pushes in....and in effect alternating.    BUT if the whole aspect of the reed up and exposed to the breeze......you'd want a stiffness as not to buckle in higher pressure wind.....so you might have to create a fixture as a housing to hold the mechanism as a whole where it can be raised and lowered into the fixture to control the length of the reed being exposed to the breeze.    SO its a question of if a grass blade type or if a bird feather type would be most effective and sensitive to be a driver.  >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>I deal with musical stuff and primarily guitar...electric guitar.  SO there is a similarity to a steel string vibrating over a pickup to induce a voltage signal to drive an amplifier......all in microvolts with a higher voltage being produced with a harder string movement.   AND almost thinking wind chimes.....

author: @kde5fan737
text:
I'm pitcturing something like a 3/4 to 1" diameter aluminum tube that is cut down the length into 2 or 3 pieces, so you have an arched shape "stick".  I have about 500-100 of these 1" tubes, all 4ft long with 1.5mm wall thickness, so fairly rigid.  

If you could make it similar to a weather vane, where it turns to face the wind, you could maximize the surface area to face the wind.  I'm also wondering if tapering the stick so it is wider the further away from the inductor, if it would not be more efficient (less mass to move at the lower ends and higher surface area at the highest part - giving a much better force to weight distribution in moving the inductor. You could even cut the tubing in V shapes, to get the top part to be wider, and also the curvature of the tubing (arch) would give it structural rigidity.  

This type of application woudl be great at remote locations where there is lots of time for a battery to be charged in between uses, like an emergancy cabin, alpine chalet or even a boat sailing the ocean as it coudl work 24/7 (assuming there is wind) and could be used to generate electricity for things like LED lights or even charge batteries (with enough units when the sails are up.

I'd really like for people to look into some kind of wind powered electrical generator for sail boats that can be used when the boat is not sailing or even when there is too much wind for the sails.   There are many times when sailes have to be lowered b/c the wind is too strong, but if they could somehow harnass that wind during these periods (or while anchored at night), it could be helpful in extending the range and number of days the crew can live on board.

author: @asbjo
text:
I don't know how to feel about this. 
While the tech is all sound and it works, I would argue that the practicality of this is very limited. 
I have many points of criticisms on the Vortex Bladeless. 
1) I would think that repeated strain on the central component would lead to material-fatigue. Especially if resonant operation is desired. I know that this is probably the weakest point of criticism, but if you want to scale this up to any reasonable size, the strain-forces become.. Significant. 
2) So. On the actual energy generation. If you could make permanent magnets with extremely high field-strengths then the generator near the bottom of the generator may start to be viable.. but I just don't see that mechanism approach anywhere near the efficiencies of conventional generators.
3) This project is old. The company behind it has received millions of Euros and has been working on it since, at least 2015. They have repeatedly delayed  their goals, lessened their generator specs. In 2017 they claimed they where going to make a 1 MW generator, and had previously claimed even more. Currently they are claiming their turbines does the following: 
"With our 80cm high devices, we are getting around 2-3W, but a 275cm high device is expected to provide 100W, and a 10-12m high one around 1kW." ( Ask for source.. YT comments and links are not mixing well )
So. firstly, they've diminished their specs over time, fine. That can happen for good reason. But then claim these numbers.. Lets take and calculate the energy/generator length. 
2.5W/0.8m = 3.125 W/m
100W/2.75m = 36 W/m
1000W/11m = 90.9 W/m
While there is nothing specifically wrong or suspicious about these numbers, as the turbines (obviously) aren't one dimensional, but three-dimensional and with length, area goes up squared.. But still. Something feels odd about those numbers. 
lets assume the 0.8m model has a width of 0.1m. the cross section the wind sees would then be 0.1m * 0.8 = 0.08m² | That would mean 2.5W/0.08m² = 31.25W/m²
And assuming the 8 to 1 ratio is the same for all the models: 2.75/8 = 0.34 | 2.75m * 0.34m = 0.95m² | 100W/0.95m² = 106W/m²
An lastly the 11m model: 11/8=1.38 | 11m*1.38m = 15m² | 1000W/15m² =  66.7W/m²
Ok. So just a quick disclaimer here. These calculations are extremely rudimentary, and I'm not claiming that those calculations reflect the actual numbers, but lets compare to a comparable vertical turbine design. 
Search on Wikipedia for "Quietrevolution wind turbine"
Rating: 6 kW 
Size 3.0m * 5.0m = 15m² capture area. 
How neat. About the same capture area as the 11m vortex bladeless. So. 6000W/15m² = 400W/m² 
And the Quietrevolution design isn't even that efficient. 
Anyways. My point is more that Vortex Bladeless claims their design is cheap, and easy to implement. Despite this, they've spent 5 years and millions of euros and STILL has no product on the market. If the damn thing is so simple, and so cheap, why hasn't it been able to just be build, sold and stand on its own in the market-place? Also, the ambiguity around the technical aspects of it also makes me uncomfortable. Also, why drop the plans for 1MW+ generators? Is it because vibrations become difficult to handle at those power levels? More foundation needed to anchor the generator when it is resonating at peak wind-speed, making the whole thing more expensive and non-viable?
Why the differences in the W/m² figures? I mean, the principle is the same, you would imagine that it scales relatively linearly with capture-area. 
I dunno. I've gone on far too long, and I may have made mistakes in my calculations. Again, I'm not saying the operational principles are bogus. They are clearly valid and sound. But the practicality of this, outside small, low-power applications, just don't seem to  be of any practical use. And what I find most repulsive about this, is they are leeching on public funds and in investors to keep this going.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
The wonderful thing about a vibrating pole rather than a wind turbine is that it is a lot less likely to chop migrating birds to bits. A similar problem exists for under sea water turbines, chopping up fish. Perhaps the vibrating reed solution could be applied in water.

Round metal chimneys are fitted with a spiral of anti Ovaling fins to stop the structure vibrating to destruction in the wind. Basically a round chimney will enter modes of oscillation where it ovals in the x and y direction, potentially resulting in the structure failing due to repetitive strain. Being round, the phenomena is omnidirectional. Unfortunately the process is one of resonance with wind speed. But the energy produced is considerable, ripping the think metal walls to bits. Maybe this force could be harnessed for power generation using a pneumatic pump and internal flexible vertical tubes within a chimney structure. I suspect that the problem with such generates is one of impedance matching for maximum energy transfer. The impedance is not necessarily electrical, it could be mechanical.

replies.text:
Do you think he's measuring AC or DC? The actual output from the speaker would be some messy kind of wave, probably difficult for the multimeter to accurately measure.

He's also measuring open circuit voltage, so there's almost zero current. Even 12V at almost zero current is almost zero power generated. 12V x ~0A = ~0W

replies.author: @iamdave84
replies.text:
You can plug headphones into a microphone jack and speak loudly into them and it's picked up like a really terrible microphone. That's because the speakers on the headphones generate electricity when the diaphragm moves back and forth.

Most (all?) devices that turn electrical energy into mechanical energy will generate electricity to some extent when you apply the right mechanical energy. They're not designed for this so are usually very inefficient at creating electricity.

replies.author: @iamdave84
replies.text:
Agreed. Any power generated at the top side of the coil would be almost completely negated by the inverse of it from the bottom side of the coil. 

If the speaker was placed at the bottom of the reed and the reed was fixed so it could see-saw around a third of the way up then the movement imparted to the top and bottom of the coil would be similar and would take advantage of leverage to move the coil with more force.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Hmm, generating electricity without movement?
Is that actually possible given the fact that the wind is moving?

If I recall correctly, there is a method of running electricity through a series of horizontal wires that will cause air movement. Perhaps they figured out how to use that in reverse to generate electricity?

Another method I am aware of are micro-wires that will generate electricity when they move. Any vibrating surface is sufficient for them to move. Technically, that is not producing electricity without movement but it would certainly appear to do so given the fact that you cannot see those micro-wires without excessively expensive, special equipment.

replies.author: @eclecticcyclist
replies.text: Yes, an inductor generates energy because of a moving magnetic field. The way this works is that the blade is moving the magnet. If the pivot point is the inductor, then you're cancelling out most of the movement in one direction with movement in the other direction. What you need is a blade with an inductor at each end so that it acts like a piston, or better still a large diaphragm speaker. Try mounting a base shaker to the wall of a shed.

author: @AntCooke
text:
Really interesting! I’ve been wondering about alternative ways of generating electricity from wind for a while now. I love turbines but they can be noisy. One idea I had was using the movement of a tree swaying in the wind to pull a line of magnets through a tube with coils around it. 

A bit of paracord from the top of the tree would pull the magnets through the tube and a spring to pull them back. 
Only problem is that I’m afraid of heights!

replies.author: @peterfitzpatrick7032
replies.text: A piezo crystal wouldn't produce high voltages, it would simply be a high impedance source. The speaker , on the other hand, is low impedance which is more useful from a power production standpoint... to extract max. energy from these systems you need matching source/load impedances ... put a low impedance load on that crystal and you'll get virtually nothing out due to the mismatch ... 🤔

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering How much would the cost/watt be? Because the reason why we are building those huge towers for wind turbines is that while a small one is fine for a home owner it is not as economical to build a million 500W microturbines as twenty 25MW ones. Given these tiny devices would likely need to be assembled by hand it seems like you need some minimum size to make them cost effective.
Good news is the upper part can be a piece of plastic pipe or an inflated balloon or whatever, no high tech or expensive materials needed.

replies.author: @RobertLowery
replies.text: @Ludifant Most microphones are going to be connected to an amplifier. In the case of your DS, phone, etc, the amplifier is going to be drawing power from the battery to boost the signal enough to make it usable for audio. I am not implying that your idea will not work though, because speakers and microphones work on the same basic principle of using coils of wire and permanent magnets to generate electrical signals.
